title: Fitting a cat flap
subtitle: Mark the opening, cut through the door, set and seal the frame
excerpt: How to fit a cat flap into a timber door: marking the cutout, drilling the corner holes, cutting with a jigsaw, setting the inner and outer frames, and sealing against draughts.
sourceNotes: Manufacturer fitting guidance (generic, not brand-specific). Collins Complete DIY Manual.
aliases: installing a cat flap in a door

glossaryTerms.definition: A reciprocating saw blade for cutting timber. T-shank blades are the standard fitting for modern jigsaws. Medium-tooth blades (6 to 8 TPI) give a clean cut in door-thickness timber.

content.text: Fitting a cat flap into a timber external door takes under two hours. The critical step is positioning the cutout: the flap must be low enough for the cat's comfortable entry but not so low that the bottom of the opening is in the door rail (the horizontal structural member). Most standard cat flaps specify a minimum sill height of 100 mm above the floor and a maximum of 250 mm.
Choose a flap rated for the door thickness (most timber external doors are 44 mm). Magnetic or microchip-reading flaps add cost but prevent neighbouring cats entering.
Cordless drill with flat bit sized to the template corner radius.
Cutting template (supplied with most cat flaps).

Position the cat flap so the bottom of the opening is 150 to 200 mm above floor level (about knee height on a domestic cat). On a panelled door, choose a panel section rather than cutting through a stile or rail.
Tape the cutting template to the door face at the chosen position. Mark around the template with a pencil. Mark the centre of each corner curve for the drill holes.
Drill a flat-bit hole at each corner mark, sized to match the corner radius of the cutout. These holes allow the
to enter and change direction at the corners.

Insert the jigsaw blade into one of the corner holes. Cut along the marked line between corner holes, working around the cutout. Keep the jigsaw baseplate flat on the door face; angling it will cause the cut to be out of square and the flap frame will not seat properly.
Step 5, sand and seal the cut edges.

Sand the cut edges smooth with 120-grit on the sanding block. Apply two coats of exterior primer to the bare wood on the cut edges; bare cut edges on an exterior door will absorb water and begin to swell and rot within a few seasons if left unprimed.

Feed the inner frame unit through the opening from the inside face. Align the outer frame on the outside and press both frames together. Fit the screws supplied with the flap through from the inside frame to the outer frame. Apply flexible sealant between the outer frame and the door face to prevent draughts.

attrs.symptom: Draughts around the frame after fitting
attrs.cause: Sealant not applied between frame and door, or flap tunnel is too short for the door thickness.
attrs.fix: For short tunnels: fit a flap with a longer tunnel for the door thickness. For sealant gaps: apply a bead of external-grade flexible sealant around the outer frame face.

attrs.symptom: Cut-out is slightly out of square
attrs.fix: Sand back the out-of-square edge with a coarse sanding block to square it up. Most cat-flap frames are wide enough to cover a small deviation.
